steps.instruction: How factors tell you where a parabola crosses the x-axis

slides.title: Factors give the x-intercepts
slides.body: A parabola crosses the x-axis where y = 0. A product equals 0 only when one of its factors is 0. So for y = (x − 1)(x + 2), set each factor to 0: x − 1 = 0 gives x = 1, and x + 2 = 0 gives x = −2. Those are the x-intercepts (the roots).

slides.title: Flip the sign of the number
slides.body: Each root is the opposite sign of the number in the factor. (x − 1) gives the root x = +1, and (x + 2) gives x = −2. So the factors point straight to where the curve hits the x-axis.

slides.title: Up or down?
slides.body: If the quadratic is positive out front, the parabola opens upward (a smile). A negative in front, like y = −(x − 1)(x + 2), flips it to open downward (a frown). The x-intercepts stay the same either way.

explanations.correct: Now pick the graph whose x-intercepts match the factors!
Roots at x = 1 and x = −2, opening upward.
explanations.incomplete: Tap the graph that matches the equation.
explanations.generic_wrong: Set each factor to zero: x − 1 = 0 → x = 1, and x + 2 = 0 → x = −2.

explanations.correct: Zeros at x = −1 and x = 3, parabola opens up.
explanations.incomplete: Select one of the four graphs.
explanations.generic_wrong: Set each factor to zero: x + 1 = 0 → x = −1, and x − 3 = 0 → x = 3.

explanations.correct: Both factors are positive subtractions, so the roots are x = 2 and x = 1.
explanations.incomplete: Pick the graph that crosses at x = 1 and x = 2.
explanations.generic_wrong: x − 2 = 0 → x = 2, and x − 1 = 0 → x = 1. Opens up.

explanations.correct: Both factors add, so the roots are negative: x = −2 and x = −3.
explanations.incomplete: Choose the graph with roots at x = −2 and x = −3.

steps.instruction: Watch the sign! Which graph shows y = −(x − 1)(x + 2)?

explanations.correct: Same roots (x = 1 and x = −2), but the minus sign flips it to open downward.
explanations.generic_wrong: Roots are still x = 1 and x = −2, but the leading − makes it open down.
